coredata.dc:title: Modeling the interplay between aging and thermal runaway propagation in large-format lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yang
Raymand, David
Guo, Wendi
Brandell, Daniel
coredata.dc:description: Thermal runaway (TR) and its propagation (TRP) pose critical risks in the application of large-format lithium-ion batteries in heavy-duty electric vehicles. In this work, we apply a computational approach using a lumped heat release model. This model is calibrated with experimental data from accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and TRP tests to investigate battery aging effects on TR and TRP. It is seen that the simulations can effectively reproduce key experimental observations, such as TR onset temperature, maximum temperature, and TRP time. Furthermore, the influence of battery aging on TR behavior is investigated, specifically solid–electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth and electrolyte degradation. The findings reveal that aging significantly accelerates TR onset while lowering the heat release of batteries. The interplay between accelerated SEI layer growth and electrolyte degradation significantly influences TRP dynamics. Compared to new batteries, the total TRP time initially decreases during early aging, reaching 78% of the original TRP time at around 80% state of health (SOH). During late aging, TRP time slightly increases to 85% of the original time at 50% SOH. This computational approach provides crucial insights into the dynamic safety of aged batteries with regard to different combinations of electrolyte degradation and SEI thickness growth rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermal runaway
Thermal runaway propagation
Battery aging
SEI layer growth
Electrolyte degradation
Computational simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reaction between the negative electrode and electrolyte
Reaction between the positive electrode and electrolyte

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All simulations in this study were performed using the COMSOL Multiphysics\u00AE software (COMSOL AB, Stockholm, Sweden). We acknowledge support from the Swedish Energy Agency, Sweden (project no. 51787-1), Batteries Sweden (Vinnova grant no. 2019-00064 and 2024-03853 ) and the STandUP for Energy and Compel consortia . Special thanks to our colleagues from Traton Group R&D: Dr. Shruti Srivastav for her valuable assistance with software setup, Dr. Vigneshwaran Thangavel for his supportive troubleshooting of the simulation model, and Dr. Litao Yin for generously sharing her expertise on battery simulation and aging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The system of extended volume accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC); (b) The test vessel with specially tailored fixture for the tested prismatic battery cells; (c) Flow chart of ARC testing protocol; (d) Schematic of thermal runaway propagation (TRP) test setup in the three-cell stack.

Overview of SEI growth and electrolyte retention during battery aging; data taken from literature [20,25,48,50–54]. The solid dots indicate experimental results, while the open circles represent estimations derived from the simulation studies. The shaded area represents a confidence interval of 95%. Extrapolation to different cell designs and operating conditions should be considered with caution unless supported by additional data.

(a) Temperature profiles of single-cell simulation and ARC test; (b) Temperature increase rate of single-cell simulation and ARC test; (c) Temperature profiles of Cell no. 1, Cell no. 2, and Cell no. 3 during thermal runaway propagation.

(a) Thermal runaway temperature profile of aged battery cells; (b) Heat sources of these aged cells from different reactions and from an internal short circuit; (c) Temperature profiles of Cell no. 1, Cell no. 2, and Cell no. 3 during thermal runaway propagation. The solid curves represent fresh battery cells with 100% SOH. The dashed and dotted curves represent aged cells with 80% and 50% SOH, respectively.

Time of TR onset from Cell no. 1 to Cell no. 3 during TRP across various SOH levels. (a-1) Fast SEI growth with slow electrolyte decomposition; (a-2) Fast SEI growth with moderate electrolyte decomposition; (a-3) Fast SEI growth with fast electrolyte decomposition; (b-1) Moderate SEI growth with slow electrolyte decomposition; (b-2) Moderate SEI growth with moderate electrolyte decomposition; (b-3) Moderate SEI growth with fast electrolyte decomposition; (c-1) Slow SEI growth with slow electrolyte decomposition; (c-2) Slow SEI growth with moderate electrolyte decomposition; (c-3) Slow SEI growth with fast electrolyte decomposition.

Physical and kinetic parameters for the four chemical reactions [29–34,42,43]. These experimentally validated kinetic parameters were adopted from literature sources. The mass fractions of the two electrode materials (

and
W
p o s
) are evaluated by the energy density of graphite anode [45] and NMC811 cathode [44].

head.ce:title: Modeling the interplay between aging and thermal runaway propagation in large-format lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Södertälje Technical Centre, Traton AB, Granparksvägen 10, 151 48 Södertälje, Sweden
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Södertälje Technical Centre, Traton AB, Granparksvägen 10, 151 48 Södertälje, Sweden

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry - Å ngström Laboratory, Uppsala University, Box 358, 751 21 Uppsala, Sweden

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry - Å ngström Laboratory, Uppsala University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry - Ångström Laboratory, Uppsala University, Box 358, 751 21 Uppsala, Sweden

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thermal runaway (TR) and its propagation (TRP) pose critical risks in the application of large-format lithium-ion batteries in heavy-duty electric vehicles. In this work, we apply a computational approach using a lumped heat release model. This model is calibrated with experimental data from accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and TRP tests to investigate battery aging effects on TR and TRP. It is seen that the simulations can effectively reproduce key experimental observations, such as TR onset temperature, maximum temperature, and TRP time. Furthermore, the influence of battery aging on TR behavior is investigated, specifically solid–electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth and electrolyte degradation. The findings reveal that aging significantly accelerates TR onset while lowering the heat release of batteries. The interplay between accelerated SEI layer growth and electrolyte degradation significantly influences TRP dynamics. Compared to new batteries, the total TRP time initially decreases during early aging, reaching 78% of the original TRP time at around 80% state of health (SOH). During late aging, TRP time slightly increases to 85% of the original time at 50% SOH. This computational approach provides crucial insights into the dynamic safety of aged batteries with regard to different combinations of electrolyte degradation and SEI thickness growth rate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing climate concerns have led to stricter CO2 regulations, including a 2035 EU mandate for all new passenger cars, vans, and urban buses to be zero-emission, and a 90% cut of emissions for heavy-duty vehicles by 2040 [1]. Electric vehicles (EVs) are central to achieving these targets and enabling a low-carbon transport future. In this context, large-format lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are essential for heavy-duty vehicles because of their high energy density. Thermal runaway (TR) can cause the risk of fires and explosions due to a tremendous amount of heat generation and combustible gas release [2]. Larger battery cells can release more heat and gas during TR, resulting in increased risks. The cells are usually arranged into battery modules and subsequently into packs. Therefore, thermal runaway propagation (TRP) at the module and pack level is extremely hazardous, as it multiplies the effects of TR and causes a significant threat to the safety of EV users [3]. These risks need to be understood and mitigated to accelerate the green transition of the EV sector.

To better understand battery TR, we have previously developed a variety of experimental approaches, including multidimensional measurements of TR in cells and stacks, and TR characterization through TRP testing in battery modules [4–9]. These experimental investigations are essential for studying the underlying mechanisms of TRP and for developing practical design guidelines that ensure safe operation [10]. However, TR testing requires significant time and financial resources and produces toxic gas during the testing process [11,12]. Testing large-format batteries is especially demanding due to their high energy release, which requires specific setups such as extended volume accelerating rate calorimetry (EV-ARC). Venting becomes especially critical in large cells, as it can account for about 67% of total heat release [13]. This complicates experimental control and repeatability; increasing the cost, time, and environmental impact of TR testing. Therefore, numerical simulations have emerged as a powerful tool that reduces reliance on trial-and-error methods and addresses challenges that are otherwise time- and resource-intensive to explore experimentally. This is especially beneficial in fields where experimental investigations are costly, slow, or challenging to control precisely. One such field is battery aging, which requires extensive cycling.

Simulations of battery aging can provide valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms of complex aging modes. Typically, battery aging is categorized into two main modes, i.e., loss of active material (LAM) and loss of lithium inventory (LLI) [14,15]. These are the main contributors to the degradation of battery capacity, characterized by the state of health (SOH). In LiNixMnyCozO2 (NMC)–graphite batteries, LAM is mainly driven by particle cracking in the negative electrode, as well as from transition metal dissolution and structural phase transformations occurring in the positive electrode [16–19]. LLI, in turn, is mainly caused by side reactions such as solid–electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth and lithium plating and is also related to particle cracking in the electrodes [20]. Plated lithium is highly reactive with the electrolyte, reducing the thermal stability of aged batteries [21,22]. SEI growth, in turn, is particularly relevant to battery safety, as its thermochemical decomposition is usually the initiating reaction in the TR chain reactions [23].

The SEI growth rate declines with increasing cycle number, exhibiting a square-root-of-time dependency [20,24–26]. This behavior occurs because the progressively thicker SEI layer restricts the transport of reactive species, leading to diffusion-controlled growth [27]. At the same time, the electrolyte is consumed during aging, which will also affect the TR behavior. This consumption is mainly driven by SEI growth, with a minor contribution from lithium plating [20,25].

Considering the complexity of both TR behavior and battery aging, their interplay needs further investigation. Here, TR simulations can be beneficial for understanding the influence of battery aging characteristics on TR behavior, which cannot be evaluated in experimental studies. Modeling can provide insights into internal phenomena such as heat generation, gas generation, and the dynamic processes of electrochemical reactions [28]. These complex chain reactions include SEI decomposition, negative electrode–electrolyte reactions, positive electrode–electrolyte reactions, and electrolyte decomposition [29–34]. The direct observation of these reactions occurring during TR is challenging through experimental measures. Therefore, physics-based simulations are likely to play a crucial role in understanding how these underlying mechanisms affect TR. Yet another advantage of computational simulations is their ability to predict the aging features of the battery that prevent TRP, allowing for the design of safer batteries and battery components [35]. These include, for example, the selection of the battery thermal pad.

In this study, a TR model from previous studies is implemented in a lumped-geometry cell, and an extended TRP model is developed for three-cell stacks comprising prismatic NMC811 batteries with a capacity of 157 Ah [29–33,36]. The model is calibrated using experimental data obtained from ARC and TRP tests, some of which have been analyzed in an earlier study [37]. The heat release from different sources, including internal short circuits (ISCs) and chemical reactions, is analyzed using the model.

The calorimeter, shown in Fig. 1(a), is an EV-ARC system manufactured by Thermal Hazard Technology. Compared to a standard ARC, the calorimeter with extended volume allows for testing large-format battery cells. Fig. 1(b) shows the specially tailored test vessel for the studied prismatic cell to accommodate its size and shape.

A commercial prismatic battery cell co-developed by Northvolt and Scania was used in the ARC test. The cell, with an NMC811 cathode and a graphite anode, has a nominal voltage of 3.6 V and a capacity of 157 Ah. This is one of the largest NMC cells reported in the ARC literature to date, which is particularly challenging due to the size and capacity limitations of the ARC device [38–40].

As presented in Fig. 1(c), the testing procedure includes a cyclic heat-wait-seek (HWS) protocol, which essentially provides accurate temperature measurements through slow heating. In the heating mode, the calorimeter heats the battery cell with a temperature increase step of 5 K at a time. The following is the waiting mode, in which the test vessel temperature is maintained for 60 min to ensure that all battery materials are evenly heated while the calorimeter searches for the onset of battery self-heating with a predefined exothermic threshold of 0.02 °C/min. When this threshold is reached, an exothermic mode is activated in the calorimeter, providing adiabatic conditions. Under such adiabatic conditions, no heat exchange can occur between the battery and the environment until TR is triggered. The temperature profile is measured on the wide lateral surfaces of the cell.

In previous work, we have studied TRP by experimental testing [4,5,37]. Fig. 1(d) illustrates the configuration of the battery cell stack. TR is triggered on Cell no. 1 with lateral heating suggested by safety standard GB38031 [41], and TR propagates along the cell stack through the battery thermal pads. The in-situ thermal conductivity obtained in that study was employed in the TRP simulations here to validate the model [37].

There exists a framework of TR models in the literature that can be utilized for further studies. One model was originally developed by Hatchard et al. to predict oven testing of LIBs [33]. Spotnitz et al. further advanced the model by identifying potential exothermic reactions during TR and defining reaction kinetics based on ARC and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) data. These TR reactions include the decomposition of metastable components in the SEI, the reaction between the intercalated lithium and electrolyte and fluorinated binder, electrolyte decomposition, the decomposition of the positive electrode, and the reactions of lithium metal from plating [34]. Kim et al. developed this model by considering the relevant reactions to simulate abuse tests of LIBs that involve no significant lithium plating or flame formation during TR [31]. This model has since been widely adopted in related studies, with parameters adjusted to suit specific research objectives [29,30,32,42,43]. The widespread application of this model demonstrates its versatility, as it generalizes TR reactions and allows for straightforward parameterization. This can be achieved by fitting the model to experimental results, such as ARC and TRP tests. Therefore, it is also adopted in this present study. Since the model does not explicitly include lithium plating as a dynamic process, it is less suited for scenarios where plating is significant.

In this model, the physical properties of the battery are taken from the cell characterized by ARC testing and are listed in Table 1. The cell temperature evolution is governed by the net heat accumulation rate from the different heat-generation and heat-loss terms.
(1)
d T
d t

Q
t o t
m ⋅ C p
where
Q
t o t
is the total heat accumulation rate,
d T
d t
denotes the temperature increase rate, m is the mass of the battery, and
C p
is the specific heat of the battery.

The total heat accumulation rate in the cell includes the heat generation rate from ISC and chemical reactions, and the heat dissipation rate to the ambient environment:
(2)
Q
t o t
=
Q
i s c
+
Q

Q
d i s s i p a t i o n
where
Q
i s c
is the electrical heat generation rate from ISC,
Q
c h e m
is the heat generation rate from chemical reactions, and
Q
d i s s i p a t i o n
is the heat dissipation rate to the ambient environment. An ISC event releases the electrical energy stored in the battery cell. The chemical reactions here, as identified in some previous studies with similar battery chemistry, include the decomposition of the SEI, the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte, the reaction between the cathode and the electrolyte, and the decomposition of the electrolyte [29–33,36]. Heat dissipation includes gas venting, where the gas removes a significant amount of heat from the cell, convection of the air that dissipates the heat from the cell surface, and radiation of the cell to the ambient environment.

The heat generation during ISC is released over a short period of time by joule heating. Here, only ISC is considered because no external circuit exists in the performed ARC test.
(3)
Q
i s c
=
E
b a t
⋅ S O H
Δ
t
i s c
where
E
b a t
is the total electrical energy stored in a fully charged battery, evaluated by its nominal capacity and voltage.
S O H
specifies the remaining capacity in the aged cell.
Δ
t
i s c
is the duration of the ISC during which TR occurs. In this study, the duration is set to 30 s based on experimental observations showing that for this cell, typically rises in an exponential trend over a period of approximately 30 s, c.f. Fig. 3(c).

The heat release rate from the chemical reactions considered includes four sources: SEI decomposition, negative electrode–electrolyte reactions, positive electrode–electrolyte reactions, and electrolyte decomposition (see Eq. (4)):
(4)

is the heat generation rate from electrolyte decomposition. The kinetics of each reaction are listed in Table 2 and are calculated using the Arrhenius equation [29–32,36].

is the density of the jelly rolls;

is the normalized concentration of the SEI. This reflects the amount of SEI materials involved in the decomposition reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction between the negative electrode and electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para:
After the decomposition of SEI, the intercalated lithium in the negative electrode reacts with the electrolyte solvent at the interface. The heat release rate from this reaction is calculated by Eq. (8):
(8)
Q

is the dimensionless thickness of SEI layer that reflects the amount of lithium in the SEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction between the positive electrode and electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para:
At high temperatures, the active materials in the positive electrode can be directly decomposed with an exothermic reaction and react with the solvent in the electrolyte. The heat release rate from both reactions is calculated by Eq. (11):
(11)
Q

where
W
p o s
is the mass proportion of the active positive electrode material in jelly rolls;
H
p e
is the heat release of the reaction between the positive electrode and electrolyte;
R
p e
is the reaction rate of the reaction between the positive electrode and electrolyte;
A
p e
is the frequency factor of the reaction between the negative electrode and electrolyte; α is the degree of conversion of the positive electrode; and
E
a , p e
is the activation energy of the reaction between the positive electrode and electrolyte.

As the electrolyte is consumed primarily by SEI growth during battery aging, it can be assumed that the electrolyte degrades at the same rate as the SEI grows. Thereby, less electrolyte will be available for decomposition in an aged battery cell than in a fresh counterpart. The heat release rate from electrolyte decomposition is calculated by Eq. (14):
(14)
Q

where
W
e l e
is the mass proportion of the electrolyte in the jelly roll, and is assumed to decrease with the decrease in SOH [48,49];
H
e l e
is the heat release from the electrolyte decomposition;
R
e l e
is the reaction rate of electrolyte decomposition;
A
e l e
is the frequency factor of electrolyte decomposition;
E
a , e l e
is the activation energy of electrolyte decomposition;
c
e l e
is the normalized concentration of the electrolyte (see Table 1).

During venting, a substantial amount of heat is ejected along with the gases and possibly solid or liquid mass. As a result, heat dissipation mechanisms during TR include (i) intense venting-driven heat loss, (ii) convective cooling to the ambient environment, and (iii) radiative heat transfer from the hot cell surface to the surroundings.
(17)
Q

where r is the ratio of venting heat dissipation to total heat generation, which is set to be 0.67 from experimentally established data [13,37],
Δ T
is the temperature difference between the battery surfaces and the ambient environment,

is the convection coefficient, A is the area of the battery surfaces, ɛ is the surface emissivity of the battery, σ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant (

is the battery surface temperature (see Table 2).

The aging model includes SEI thickness (
d
S E I
) and electrolyte retention (
f
e l e c t r o l y t e
) as functions of SOH based on previous studies, as summarized in Fig. 2. This empirical model provides a representation of the available data and effectively reproduces the trends of SEI growth and electrolyte degradation [20,25,48,50–54]. The highlighted bands in Fig. 2 represent the 95% confidence interval (CI) of the fitted regression curves, i.e., the uncertainty of the estimated mean response. The available data are expected to scatter around the mean due to various aging conditions. Accordingly, for a given SOH, the model provides a best estimate of SEI and electrolyte retention together with a uncertainty range. Their 95% CI boundaries are used to represent moderate, fast, and slow changes in SEI and electrolyte. The equations for these curves are expressed in a square-root function, with their parameters determined through fitting, and are presented as Eq. (21) and (22). All pairwise combinations of SEI growth and electrolyte degradation are generated, resulting in nine different cases to simulate various battery aging scenarios. These scenarios account for the interdependence between SEI growth and electrolyte degradation and provide valuable insights into how changes of SEI and electrolyte affect battery safety during aging.

(21)
d
S E I
= 10 + α
100 % − S O H
(22)
f
e l e c t r o l y t e
= 100 − β
100 % − S O H
where
d
S E I
denotes the SEI thickness (nm), the initial SEI thickness is 10 nm [51], and α is the coefficient governing the SEI growth rate with fitted values of 27.56 for fast growth, 20.95 for moderate growth, and 14.35 for slow growth;
f
e l e c t r o l y t e
represents the electrolyte retention (%), and β is the coefficient governing the electrolyte degradation rate, with fitted values of 8.86 for fast decomposition, 6.47 for moderate decomposition, and 4.08 for slow decomposition. The values of α and β were determined based on the 95% CI, and represent the sensitivity analysis.

Fig. 3(a) shows the simulated temperature profile during TR at the single-cell level and the results of ARC testing. As can be seen, the model can accurately capture the TR onset temperature, the maximum temperature, and the temperature decrease rate after reaching its peak temperature. This alignment validates the model in simulating these critical characteristics of TR.

Fig. 3(b) shows the change in temperature increase rate, which signifies the intensity of the TR. It can be seen from the ARC results that the cell starts to heat up steadily from around 100 °C, signifying the onset of self-heating. At the same time, the simulation results show a slight temperature fluctuation at the same temperature. This indicates that exothermic reactions have started at 100 °C according to the model. At 155 °C, both curves exhibit an exponential rise in temperature increase rate, indicating the onset of TR. Thereafter, both temperature increase rates achieve their peak at above 100 K/s. Such a similarity again highlights the accuracy and validity of the computational model in relation to the experimental results.

Fig. 3(c) represents the simulation and experimental results of TRP through the module. Although the lumped model does not resolve internal temperature gradients within each cell, its baseline response is validated by showing good agreement in onset temperature, peak temperature, and TRP time. To estimate the possible discrepancy introduced by omitting internal gradients, an experimentally observed thermal lag (i.e., the time delay between TR onset at the front and back surfaces of a cell) of 39 s was considered as an uncertainty range. Accordingly, the error bars in Fig. 3(c) reflect the plausible variation in TR onset timing associated with internal non-uniformity, thereby providing an assessment of how the lumped assumption may affect the predicted TRP time.

Fig. 4(a) exhibits overheat-induced TR behavior of aged battery cells with different SOH. It can be observed that TR is triggered the fastest in the cell at 50% SOH, followed by the 80% SOH cell, with the 100% SOH cell exhibiting the slowest TR onset temperature. During TR, the 100% SOH cell reaches the highest maximum temperature, the 80% SOH cell a slightly lower value, and the 50% SOH cell the lowest – i.e., the reverse trend. It is thus demonstrated that battery aging can cause a faster TR onset but a lower maximum temperature. These findings agree well with previous experimental literature that reports lower TR onset temperature and lower maximum temperature in an aged battery than in a new battery [55–58]. The lower TR onset temperature is because the growth of SEI layer in an aged cell can produce significant heat by its decomposition. On the other hand, the decline in energy content associated with capacity fade in aged cells reduces the total heat released during TR, thereby producing a lower peak temperature compared to a new cell.

Fig. 4(b) illustrates the heat release from various chemical reactions (SEI decomposition, positive electrode–electrolyte reaction, negative electrode–electrolyte reaction, electrolyte decomposition) and ISC, revealing important internal mechanisms when the battery undergoes TR. The results show that in each aging scenario, heat release occurs initially with SEI decomposition, suggesting that chemical reactions under overheating conditions begin with this. It has been reported that the SEI starts to decompose as early as 100 °C under overheating [59]. This decomposition is an exothermic reaction that generates heat and can trigger the onset of battery self-heating, subsequently initiating other chain reactions of the TR, including the positive electrode–electrolyte reaction, negative electrode–electrolyte reaction, and electrolyte decomposition. Therefore, the thickness of the SEI layer is likely to have a strong impact on the onset of TR.

Furthermore, Fig. 4(b) demonstrates that the thickened SEI layer in aged cells (80% and 50% SOH) decomposes earlier and releases more heat compared to the new cell (100% SOH). This occurs due to the thicker SEI layer in the aged batteries, i.e. more reactive material has been formed. The decomposition of the grown SEI leads to more heat release and, thus, an earlier onset of TR in the aged cells than in fresh cells. The onset of TR occurs progressively earlier as the SOH decreases. The heat release from the positive electrode–electrolyte reaction and the decomposition of the electrolyte is lower in the aged cells compared to the fresh cell. This is due to both LAM in the positive electrode and electrolyte decomposition, resulting in less reactants in the exothermic reactions. Moreover, the electrical heat release from the ISC decreases significantly in an aged cell. The occurrence of an ISC releases the remaining electrical energy as Ohmic heat. Consequently, the decrease in SOH directly reduces the amount of stored electrical energy available for release.

Fig. 4(c) shows the effect of aging on TRP. Reduced SOH results in an accelerated onset of TR for Cell no. 1 in the module. On the other hand, the reduced SOH correlates with a decrease in the maximum temperature, likely driven by the aging-induced decrease in electrolyte content and battery capacity. The total TRP time from Cell no. 1 to Cells no. 3 declines significantly as SOH decreases from 100% to around 80% SOH, but increases moderately from 80% to 50% SOH, remaining shorter than 100% SOH. This behavior results from the combined effects of faster TR initiation due to SEI growth and the low heat release influence of electrolyte and capacity degradation. The former results in faster TRP in aged batteries, whereas the latter leads to a lower maximum TR temperature in aged batteries than in new ones.

Fig. 5 illustrates the effect of SOH on the time of TRP, considering different combinations of the SEI growth and electrolyte degradation rates. The purpose is to observe key factors that control TRP behavior, and the possibility to tailor the electrolyte system to control battery safety properties through its battery aging properties. It can be seen in all aging scenarios that the initiation of TR in Cell no. 1 occurs progressively earlier as SOH decreases, especially from 100% SOH to 99% SOH. This phenomenon demonstrates vulnerability of aged cells. However, it is indicated that the TRP time for Cells no. 2 and no. 3 initially declines but thereafter experiences a significant increase. Consequently, this behavior leads to the initial reduction and subsequent rise in total TRP time. This is due to the dominant role of rapid SEI growth in accelerating TR onset during early aging, whereas during late aging, the degradation of energy content and electrolyte becomes predominant, resulting in reduced heat generation. It is demonstrated in Fig. 4(b) that SEI decomposition occurs prior to the onset of TR, releasing heat that can trigger TR. In aged cells, the grown SEI layer leads to greater heat release from this initial decomposition, promoting an earlier TR onset. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 4(b), heat release from electrolyte decomposition and ISC is progressively less with aging due to the degradation in electrolyte and capacity. The interplay between these factors results in a U-shaped trend of TRP time, with the poorest safety performance near 80% SOH.

Fig. 5 shows that the fastest TRP occurs at around 80% SOH, where the TRP time is approximately 15%–29% shorter than at 100% SOH. At 50% SOH, the TRP time decreases by 15% compared to that of the new battery, on average. By comparing these different aging scenarios in Fig. 5, it can be observed that faster SEI growth leads to slightly earlier TR onset and that slower electrolyte decomposition consistently accelerates TRP compared to faster electrolyte decomposition. Notably, the interplay between slow SEI growth and fast electrolyte decomposition, i.e., Fig. 5(c-3), results in the longest TRP time at 50% SOH which is 4% longer than that of the new battery. These results highlight the safety dynamics during battery aging.

As a battery ages, two competing effects influence TRP time. At early aging, thickened SEI layer produces more heat during its decomposition. In contrast, at late aging, capacity and electrolyte degradation tends to produce less heat and lower the maximum temperature. The competition between these two effects naturally leads to the non-monotonic trend with SOH exhibited in the simulations.

A limitation of this study is that the simulation model does not include all aging mechanisms. There are also other aging factors affecting battery safety, such as lithium plating. Moreover, due to the complexity of testing large-scale commercial prismatic batteries, as well as the limited availability of well-characterized aged cells, direct comparison to TRP testing data on aged cells is not readily available. Although reaction kinetics may evolve with aging, aged kinetic parameters are not available for the commercial large-format cells. Therefore, the present work applies literature-based kinetics for new cells as a baseline. Future work should combine aged-cell experiments with parameter identification to constrain kinetic evolution. Nevertheless, the simulated trends are consistent with previously reported experimental findings, supporting the qualitative credibility of the results. Importantly, this study reveals the competing effects of reduced thermal stability and decreased energy content in aged batteries during TRP.

In this study, a computational approach was employed to simulate thermal runaway (TR) and its propagation (TRP) in large-format lithium-ion batteries. The model used was adapted from previous studies and was found to capture essential behavior observed experimentally through accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and TRP testing. This includes accurate predictions of the TR onset temperature, the maximum temperature, and TRP time.

It is concluded that battery safety is not governed by a single aging mechanism, but by the interplay among SEI growth, SOH decrease, and electrolyte degradation—each influenced by usage conditions and collectively shaping the thermal response of the aged cell. During early aging (100% SOH – ca. 80% SOH), SEI growth is the dominant effect that accelerates TRP by releasing heat faster. On the other hand, during late aging (SOH <
ca. 80%), the SOH decreases, and electrolyte degradation becomes the dominant effect that decelerates TRP by lowering the total heat release while accelerating TRP. The shortest TRP time occurs at approximately 80% SOH and increases slightly with further aging. The model captures key competing aging mechanisms but does not include all possible mechanisms, and limited aged-cell data restricts full validation. Nevertheless, the simulated trends align with reported observations, making the framework useful for understanding safety dynamics in aged batteries.

Yang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. David Raymand: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Wendi Guo: Writing – review & editing. Daniel Brandell: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All simulations in this study were performed using the COMSOL Multiphysics® software (COMSOL AB, Stockholm, Sweden). We acknowledge support from the Swedish Energy Agency, Sweden (project no. 51787-1), Batteries Sweden (Vinnova grant no. 2019-00064 and 2024-03853) and the STandUP for Energy and Compel consortia . Special thanks to our colleagues from Traton Group R&D: Dr. Shruti Srivastav for her valuable assistance with software setup, Dr. Vigneshwaran Thangavel for his supportive troubleshooting of the simulation model, and Dr. Litao Yin for generously sharing her expertise on battery simulation and aging.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparative investigation of aging effects on thermal runaway behavior of lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Ren, H. Hsu, R. Li, X. Feng, D. Guo, X. Han, L. Lu, X. He, S. Gao, J. Hou, Y. Li, Y. Wang, and M. Ouyang, “A comparative investigation of aging effects on thermal runaway behavior of lithium-ion batteries,” eTransportation, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal runaway of Li-ion battery with different aging histories
Process. Saf. Environ. Prot.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, L. Liu, A. Terekhov, D. Warnberg, and P. Zhao, “Thermal runaway of li-ion battery with different aging histories,” Process Safety and Environmental Protection, 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perspective – On the safety of aged lithium-ion batteries
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Preger, L. Torres-Castro, T. Rauhala, and J. Jeevarajan, “Perspective – on the safety of aged lithium-ion batteries,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery thermal safety evolution during high-temperature nonlinear aging
Fuel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Zhang, W. Shen, and X. Wei, “Lithium-ion battery thermal safety evolution during high-temperature nonlinear aging,” Fuel, 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Accelerating rate calorimetry study on the thermal stability of lithium intercalated graphite in electrolyte. I. Experimental
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Richard and J. Dahn, “Accelerating rate calorimetry study on the thermal stability of lithium intercalated graphite in electrolyte. i. experimental,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 146, no. 6, p. 2068, 1999.
